claim_text: In a reader study of 1000 MR images evaluated by five radiologists across various degradation types, Mason et al. demonstrated that NRMSE, SSIM, and PSNR have weaker concordance with radiologist-perceived diagnostic IQ than several less commonly-reported IQ metrics.

sub_claims.text: The reader study used a library of 1000 MR images.

evidence.snippet: Each degradation was applied to each reference at four different strengths. This yielded a total image library of 414 images including the reference images.

The comparison uses a database of MR images of the brain and abdomen that have been retrospectively degraded by noise, blurring, undersampling, motion, and wavelet compression for a total of 414 degraded images. A total of 1017 subjective scores were assigned by five radiologists.

sub_claims.nuance: The paper reports 414 images in the library and 1017 subjective scores assigned across them. The manuscript's '1000 MR images' may conflate the image library size with the number of ratings (1017).

sub_claims.text: The reader study was evaluated by five radiologists.

evidence.snippet: Three body radiologists and two neuro radiologists were involved in the study.
We measured the correlations between 10 full-reference objective IQMs and the subjective image quality score of five subspecialty radiologists.
we compare the image quality scores of five radiologists with the RMSE, SSIM, and other potentially useful IQMs

sub_claims.text: The reader study spanned various degradation types.

evidence.snippet: One of six degradation techniques was individually applied to each image: white Gaussian noise, Gaussian blurring, Rician noise, undersampling of k-space data, wavelet compression, and motion artifacts.
retrospectively degraded by noise, blurring, undersampling, motion, and wavelet compression

evidence.section: A. Generation of Image Library (Table I caption)
evidence.snippet: TABLE I DESCRIPTION OF THE SIX DEGRADATION TYPES USED.

sub_claims.text: NRMSE, SSIM, and PSNR have weaker concordance with radiologist-perceived diagnostic image quality than several less commonly-reported IQMs.

evidence.snippet: The SROCC of each IQM with each radiologist's scores are shown in Fig. 4. ... Note that RMSE and SSIM are among the metrics with the lowest SROCC. Overall, VIF had the highest SROCC values. ... for many of the radiologists in the study, VIF, FSIM, and NQM perform statistically better than the other IQMs included in the study. SSIM did not perform statistically better than any another IQM including RMSE.

the results of this study demonstrate that, in the retrospectively degraded images used, SSIM does not show a significantly stronger correlation with radiologist opinion of diagnostic image quality than RMSE, and that there are other objective IQMs that perform better.

When considering images divided by reference location or combining all images in the study, SSIM and RMSE demonstrated statistically worse performances than other metrics evaluated, suggesting that SSIM and RMSE are potentially not ideal surrogate measures of MR image quality as determined by radiologist evaluation. The VIF, FSIM, and NQM demonstrated the highest correlation with radiologists' opinions of MR image quality.

When considering SROCC calculated from combining scores from all radiologists across all image types, RMSE and SSIM had lower SROCC than six of the other IQMs included in the study (VIF, FSIM, NQM, GMSD, IWSSIM, and HDRVDP).

figures_checked.question: n/a — caption inspected via figures/index.json without vision call
figures_checked.vision_response: Caption: 'Spearman rank order correlation coefficient for each IQM when data is divided by radiologist (top), reference type (middle), or degradation type (bottom).' Per text in Section II.RESULTS, IQMs in this figure are sorted by decreasing SROCC and RMSE/SSIM are 'among the metrics with the lowest SROCC.'

sub_claims.paper_value: RMSE and SSIM had lower SROCC than six other IQMs (VIF, FSIM, NQM, GMSD, IWSSIM, HDRVDP); PSNR not separately identified as 'weaker' — paper uses RMSE (and PSNR is monotonic transform of RMSE).
sub_claims.claim_value: NRMSE, SSIM, and PSNR are weaker than several less commonly-reported IQMs
sub_claims.nuance: Paper uses RMSE (not NRMSE) and discusses PSNR as a monotonic transform of RMSE; claim's substitution of NRMSE/PSNR for RMSE is a paraphrase and the 'less commonly-reported' framing is the manuscript's, not the source's.

phrasings_tried: 1000 MR images / 1017 / 414 images / total image library
five radiologists / 5 radiologists / three body and two neuro / subspecialty radiologists
various degradation types / six degradation / noise blurring undersampling motion wavelet compression
RMSE SSIM PSNR weaker concordance / lower SROCC / lower correlation / less commonly-reported IQMs / VIF FSIM NQM GMSD IWSSIM HDRVDP
Spearman rank order correlation coefficient / SROCC ordering
attestation.closest_adjacent: For the '1000 MR images' sub-claim, the closest figures in the source are 414 (total image library) and 1017 (total subjective scores assigned by the five radiologists across the library). Neither equals 1000; '1017 subjective scores' is the nearest match in magnitude.
attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Mason et al. report this as their own primary reader study; they do not credit another primary source for the SROCC comparison or the image-library counts. They cite Renieblas et al. [20] for a related but smaller-scope expert-rater study (SSIM family only), explicitly distinguishing their work as broader.
attestation.out_of_context_check: The source paper presents these results as evaluating full-reference IQMs on retrospectively degraded clinically-normal MR images (no pathology), using a Likert-style diagnostic quality score as the radiologist outcome. The manuscript's framing 'radiologist-perceived diagnostic IQ' aligns with the paper's diagnostic-quality scoring. The paper's authors caution generalizability is limited (clinically normal images only; specific MRI systems/sequences); the manuscript does not flag this scope. Mild context simplification but not a misuse.
